text.fullText: "I just got finished watching the FOX news story on this, and I couldn't help but come submit a comment for myself. As an America First citizen, I found a few alarming flaws in the proposal. I think that restricting the ability of the local state and tribal governments to reject proposals to solely discharge is a bad idea, and would harm America's ability to compete in the global stage. It is unamerican to restrict a state's (or tribal government's) right to object to reject a proposal based on its holistic effects, not just the discharge. It would also reduce our competitive advantage. What makes America the best country in the world is our commitment to our constitutional values like state's rights, and our commitment to our natural resources. Going into the 21st century and beyond, it is critical that we preserve and protect our limited water supply from any pollution or holistic negative effects to maintain our global supremacy. It isn't woke to consider the total effects of a project, it is in fact anti-woke. Using facts and logic to get a full understanding of how a proposal would affect the whole of a critical resource and habitat is a rational and ultimately anti-woke endeavor. It would be a betrayal of Christian and American values to make any changes to the 2023 policy that might endanger states rights or our water supply. As a conservative, I believe in conserving our natural resources and our local governments ability to reject proposals based on all of their effects. All fellow republicans that I've talked to have agreed with me that there should be no changes to the 2023 policy, or at the least, no narrowing of the scope of state's rights to reject a proposal on it's total effect. Unfortunately, there doesn't seem to be much discourse among conservatives online about this key issue. It is striking to me that there is a significant silent moral majority of conservatives and americans at large offline who would actually be really mad if the EPA made the decision to narrow the purview of the 2023 policy. It should be taken into account when reviewing these comments the overwhelming majority of offline americans who would not support these changes, and would prefer the more american, patriotic, and conservative decision to return to the common sense of the 2023 policies, and conserve the rights of states and local tribes to review and reject policies based on their total, holistic, impact on america, american land, and our position on the global stage.
